did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a linear-kernel support vector machine (LSVM) model, a machine learning algorithm, to classify nonmelanoma skin lesions (basal cell carcinoma, actinic keratosis, and Bowen's disease) based on fluorescence lifetime imaging microscopy (FLIM) features extracted from H&E-stained biopsy sections.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and validation of a linear-kernel support vector machine (LSVM) model for classifying nonmelanoma skin lesions. It details specific methods including sample preparation from nine patients, two-photon fluorescence lifetime imaging microscopy (FLIM) data acquisition, feature extraction, and model evaluation using ROC curves and cross-validation, resulting in new diagnostic accuracy metrics.

title: Support Vector Machine Classification of Nonmelanoma Skin Lesions Based on Fluorescence Lifetime Imaging Microscopy
brief_summary: This study developed a linear-kernel support vector machine (LSVM) model to classify nonmelanoma skin lesions, specifically distinguishing basal cell carcinoma (BCC) from actinic keratosis (AK) and Bowen’s disease (BD). The model utilized fluorescence lifetime imaging microscopy (FLIM) features extracted from hematoxylin and eosin (H&E)-stained biopsy sections obtained from nine patients. Key input features included the median and width of the second fluorescence lifetime component (τ2) and Shannon entropy values. The diagnostic performance was evaluated using receiver operating characteristic (ROC) curves and 5-fold cross-validation on a dataset split into training and testing sets. The results demonstrated that incorporating τ2 and entropy significantly improved classification accuracy, achieving up to 99.3% accuracy for distinguishing BCC from benign lesions and 96.7% for distinguishing AK from BD. These findings suggest that FLIM combined with SVM classification offers a highly accurate, computer-aided diagnostic tool for dermatology.

lesion_types_studied.details: Studied as a malignant nonmelanoma skin cancer. Classified against benign lesions (AK and BD) and distinguished from them using FLIM features. Samples obtained from 2 patients.
Studied as a premalignant form of squamous cell carcinoma. Classified as a benign lesion alongside BD against BCC, and distinguished from BD. Samples obtained from 2 patients.
Studied as a nonmelanoma skin cancer precursor. Classified as a benign lesion alongside AK against BCC, and distinguished from AK. Samples obtained from 2 patients.
Mentioned in the introduction as a malignant skin lesion for context, but not studied or included in the classification experiments.
Mentioned in the introduction as a subtype of nonmelanoma skin cancer and as the origin of AK, but not directly studied or included in the classification experiments.

all_task_types.target: Diagnostic categories of nonmelanoma skin lesions (Basal Cell Carcinoma vs. Actinic Keratosis/Bowen's Disease; Actinic Keratosis vs. Bowen's Disease)
all_task_types.details: Binary classification tasks using a linear-kernel support vector machine (LSVM) to distinguish malignant lesions (BCC) from benign/premalignant lesions (AK, BD) and to distinguish between the benign subcategories (AK vs. BD). The model predicts discrete class labels based on fluorescence lifetime features and Shannon entropy extracted from FLIM images.

datasets_used.name: Shenzhen Sixth People's Hospital Skin Biopsy Cohort
datasets_used.description: A private clinical dataset consisting of fresh human skin specimens obtained from nine patients undergoing routine skin biopsies at the Department of Dermatology, Sixth People’s Hospital of Shenzhen. The samples were processed into 27 H&E-stained tissue sections (3 consecutive sections per patient) identified by a senior pathologist as AK (2 patients), BD (2 patients), and BCC (2 patients). The remaining 3 patients' samples were likely used for other lesion types or excluded from the final labeled set of 270 instances described in the results.

use.training: 135 instances were used for training the LSVM model via leave-one-out 5-fold cross-validation to determine optimal hyperparameters.
use.validation: Null (Cross-validation was used for hyperparameter tuning within the training set).
use.testing: 135 instances were used as an independent testing set to evaluate the predictive accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity of the trained models.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To determine the optimal hyperplane and penalty parameter C for the SVM model during the training stage.
To evaluate the performance of the SVM classifiers and visualize the diagnostic accuracy for binary classification tasks.
To quantify the diagnostic performance of the SVM models based on the ROC curves.
To fit the fluorescence decay histogram for each pixel using a triple-exponential components model to calculate fluorescence lifetime distribution.
To fit the lifetime histogram of τ2 for each 28 × 28 pixel patch to determine the median and width of the lifetime.

hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no significant difference in the mean fluorescence lifetime (τm) among the AK, BD, and BCC lesion types.
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There is a statistically significant difference in the mean fluorescence lifetime (τm) among the AK, BD, and BCC lesion types.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The Welch t-test revealed statistically significant differences in the mean value of τm histograms among the AK, BD, and BCC samples, indicating that these lifetime features can serve as ideal features for identifying skin neoplastic lesions.

Experiment_1_BCC_vs_Benign_tau_m.description: LSVM model using median (μ) and width of average fluorescence lifetime (τm) to distinguish BCC from AK and BD
Experiment_1_BCC_vs_Benign_tau_m.notes: Failed to distinguish between AK and BD subcategories.

Experiment_2_AK_vs_BD_tau_m.description: LSVM model using median (μ) and width of average fluorescence lifetime (τm) to distinguish AK from BD

LSVM (τm features).optimal_C: Not specified (tuning did not effectively increase performance)

Experiment_2_AK_vs_BD_tau_m.notes: Tuning the penalty parameter C value did not effectively increase the performance.

Experiment_3_BCC_vs_Benign_tau2.description: LSVM model using median (μ) and width of second fluorescence lifetime component (τ2) to distinguish BCC from AK and BD
Experiment_3_BCC_vs_Benign_tau2.notes: Achieved better classification performance than τm model with narrower margin and fewer support vectors.

Experiment_4_AK_vs_BD_tau2.description: LSVM model using median (μ) and width of second fluorescence lifetime component (τ2) to distinguish AK from BD
Experiment_4_AK_vs_BD_tau2.notes: Data points of τ2 corresponding to the three clusters were easily separated.

Experiment_5_BCC_vs_Benign_tau2_entropy.description: LSVM model using 3D feature space (median μ, width, and Shannon entropy) derived from τ2 to distinguish BCC from AK and BD
Experiment_5_BCC_vs_Benign_tau2_entropy.notes: Projections on the planes of entropy-μ and entropy-width could be completely separated.

Experiment_6_AK_vs_BD_tau2_entropy.description: LSVM model using 3D feature space (median μ, width, and Shannon entropy) derived from τ2 to distinguish AK from BD
Experiment_6_AK_vs_BD_tau2_entropy.notes: Incorporating Shannon entropy as an independent feature further improved the predictive accuracy.

architecture_used.input_size: Features extracted from 28 × 28 pixel patches; input feature space was 2D (median and width of lifetime) or 3D (median, width, and Shannon entropy of τ2)

architecture_details.feature_extraction_method: Fluorescence lifetime imaging microscopy (FLIM) features from H&E-stained biopsy sections. Specifically, the median (μ) and width of the second fluorescence lifetime component (τ2) were extracted by fitting the lifetime histogram of τ2 for each 28 × 28 pixel patch with a Gaussian distribution. Shannon entropy was calculated as an independent feature.

hyperparameters.penalty_parameter_C: Optimized via leave-one-out 5-fold cross-validation. Optimal C value was 2^-11 for τm features and 2^-14 for τ2 and τ2+entropy features.

training_details.optimizer: Leave-one-out 5-fold cross-validation used to determine the optimal hyperplane and penalty parameter C.
training_details.dataset_split: 270 instances total (90 per class: AK, BD, BCC); split into training set (135 instances) and testing set (135 instances).

classes: Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC) vs. Benign (AK & BD)

preprocessing_techniques: H&E staining of fresh human skin biopsy sections
Image segmentation using phasor-FLIM analysis to separate stratum corneum (SC), epithelial cells (ECs), and dermal connective tissue (CT)
Extraction of small windows (28 × 28 pixels) from the segmented epithelial cell layers
Least-squares fitting of fluorescence decay histograms using a triple-exponential components model for each pixel
Fitting of the lifetime histogram of the second component (τ2) for each 28 × 28 pixel patch with a Gaussian distribution to determine median and width
conclusions: Fluorescence lifetime imaging microscopy (FLIM) combined with linear-kernel support vector machine (LSVM) classification exhibits great potential for developing an effective computer-aided diagnostic criterion and accurate cancer detection in dermatology.

Features obtained from the second fluorescence lifetime component (τ2) are significantly more predictive than the average fluorescence lifetime (τm) in terms of diagnostic accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity for distinguishing basal cell carcinoma (BCC) from actinic keratosis (AK) and Bowen’s disease (BD).
Incorporating Shannon entropy as an independent feature alongside τ2 features further improves the diagnostic accuracy of the SVM model.
The LSVM model using τ2 and entropy features achieved a high diagnostic accuracy of 99.3% for distinguishing BCC from benign lesions (AK and BD) and 96.7% for distinguishing AK from BD.

The LSVM model using average fluorescence lifetime (τm) failed to distinguish between the AK and BD subcategories, achieving only 66.7% accuracy, whereas the τ2-based model successfully separated these clusters.
Tuning the penalty parameter C value did not effectively increase the performance of the model when using τm features for distinguishing AK from BD.
important_remarks: The study relies on a very small sample size, with tissue samples obtained from only nine patients (2 patients per lesion type: AK, BD, BCC), which limits the generalizability of the findings.
The dataset is derived from a single center (Sixth People’s Hospital of Shenzhen), introducing potential population and dataset bias; external validation on independent cohorts is not reported.

The model failed to distinguish between Actinic Keratosis (AK) and Bowen’s Disease (BD) using average fluorescence lifetime (τm) features, achieving only 66.7% accuracy, indicating that τm is insufficient for this specific differential diagnosis.

While the LSVM model using τ2 and Shannon entropy features achieved high accuracy (99.3% for BCC vs. benign; 96.7% for AK vs. BD), these results are based on a limited training/testing split (135 instances each) from a small cohort.

The study uses H&E-stained biopsy sections, which are standard for pathology, but the fluorescence lifetime features are extracted from these sections rather than fresh tissue, which may introduce artifacts or variability compared to in vivo FLIM.

The paper does not report statistical significance tests (e.g., p-values) for the classification performance differences between models, only reporting accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, and AUC values.

Data leakage risks are mitigated by the use of a separate testing set (135 instances) for final evaluation, but the small total number of patients (n=9) raises concerns about overfitting despite the use of cross-validation for hyperparameter tuning.
